title: Concrete driveway replacement cost: 2026 full breakdown
excerpt: Replacing a concrete driveway costs $7-$15 per square foot in 2026 — typically $4,500-$10,000 for a 2-car driveway including demo, haul-off, and new pour.
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed driveway, slab, patio, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, replacing a concrete driveway runs $7-$15 per square foot installed all-in (demo, haul-off, base prep, pour, and finishing). On a typical 600-square-foot 2-car driveway that's $4,200-$9,000, with $4,500-$7,500 the most common quote band. The replacement number is roughly 25-50 percent higher than a brand-new install on bare ground because demolition, haul-off, and additional base prep are not part of new-construction pricing. Demolition alone accounts for $1.50-$3.50 per square foot — most of the cost gap. Driveway replacement pricing is meaningfully lower than full repair-and-resurface combined work above roughly 30 percent slab damage; below that threshold, repair usually wins on cost.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to replace a 2-car concrete driveway in 2026?
faqs.answer: A typical 2-car driveway (600 square feet) runs $4,500-$10,000 all-in for replacement in 2026 — including demo, haul-off, new base prep, and the new pour. Most quotes come in at $4,500-$7,500. Wider driveways, longer runs to the street, decorative finishes, or sloped sites push toward the upper end. Tear-out alone is $1,500-$2,500 of that total.

faqs.question: Is it cheaper to repair or replace a concrete driveway?
faqs.answer: Repair is cheaper than replacement up to roughly 30 percent slab damage by area. Below that, crack injection, mudjacking, or partial slab replacement runs $1.50-$8 per square foot of affected area. Above 30 percent damage or where slab settlement is widespread, full replacement at $7-$15 per square foot delivers better total value because spot repairs continue to age while the unrepaired slab keeps failing.

faqs.question: How much does it cost just to remove a concrete driveway?
faqs.answer: Concrete driveway removal alone runs $1.50-$3.50 per square foot in 2026 — roughly $900-$2,100 for a 600-square-foot driveway. The pricing includes mechanical breaking (jackhammer or skid-steer breaker), hauling debris, and dump-fee disposal. Reinforced concrete (rebar, mesh) costs roughly 15-25 percent more to remove than unreinforced. Pricing rises another 20-40 percent in metros with high dump fees (San Francisco, Seattle, New York).

faqs.question: Why is replacing a driveway more expensive than installing a new one?
faqs.answer: A driveway replacement adds two cost categories that a new install doesn't have: demolition (mechanical break-up of the old slab) and haul-off disposal. Together they add $1.50-$3.50 per square foot — typically $1,000-$2,200 on a 2-car driveway. The base prep is also usually more involved on a replacement because the underlying base often needs rework after the old slab is pulled.

faqs.question: Can you pour new concrete over an old driveway?
faqs.answer: You can in some cases, with a 2-3 inch concrete overlay (resurfacing) running $4-$8 per square foot. But overlays only work if the underlying slab is structurally sound — no settlement, no major cracks, no surface contamination. Most driveways past their service life have at least one of those issues, which is why true overlays are rarer than full replacements. According to the American Concrete Institute, overlays bonded to compromised substrate typically fail within 5-8 years.

faqs.question: How long does a concrete driveway last before it needs replacement?
faqs.answer: A properly installed concrete driveway lasts 25-50 years before requiring full replacement, with 30-35 years the typical national average. Driveways in freeze-thaw climates fail earlier (25-30 years); those in warm climates with good base prep can pass 50. The Portland Cement Association cites proper base preparation and timely sealing as the two biggest determinants of whether a driveway hits the low end or high end of that range.

faqs.question: Should I add rebar when replacing my driveway?
faqs.answer: For most residential driveways, wire mesh or synthetic fiber reinforcement is the cost-effective standard. Rebar is recommended for driveways supporting heavy trucks, RVs, or boat trailers; on expansive soils; or in freeze-thaw climates. Adding rebar to a typical 600-square-foot driveway runs $300-$600 more than wire mesh and significantly reduces hairline cracking over the slab's life.

faqs.question: How long does a driveway replacement take?
faqs.answer: A standard 2-car driveway replacement takes 2-4 days of on-site work spread over 7-14 days. Day 1: demolition, haul-off, base prep. Day 2-3: forming, pour, finishing. Day 4+: cure. The slab is generally drivable by light vehicles after 7 days and full strength at 28 days. Total elapsed time from quote signature to drivable driveway is typically 3-6 weeks counting scheduling lead time.

howToSteps.name: Mark and protect surrounding area
howToSteps.text: The crew marks utility locates, protects adjacent landscaping, lawn, and curb. Plastic or plywood covers anything that could be hit by debris during the break-up phase.

howToSteps.name: Break up existing slab
howToSteps.text: The old slab is broken into manageable chunks with a jackhammer, hydraulic breaker, or skid-steer mounted breaker. Reinforcement (mesh or rebar) is exposed and cut as the slab comes apart.

howToSteps.name: Haul off debris
howToSteps.text: Concrete debris is loaded into a dump trailer or roll-off container and hauled to a recycling yard or landfill. Disposal fees vary $35-$120 per ton depending on metro and whether the concrete is clean or rebar-contaminated.

howToSteps.name: Rework base and grade
howToSteps.text: The exposed subgrade is inspected, regraded, and re-compacted. Soft spots are dug out and replaced; a 4-6 inch compacted gravel base is added or refreshed. Final grade slopes 1-2 percent away from the house for drainage.

howToSteps.name: Set forms and reinforcement
howToSteps.text: Forms are set to mark the new slab edges. Wire mesh, fiber, or rebar reinforcement is placed per the agreed spec, supported on chairs to land mid-slab during the pour.

howToSteps.name: Pour and finish
howToSteps.text: Ready-mix concrete is poured (typically 3,500-4,000 PSI for residential driveways), screeded level, bull-floated, edged, and finished — usually broom for traction, smooth trowel for sealed garage approaches, or stamped for decorative.

howToSteps.name: Cut control joints
howToSteps.text: Control joints are saw-cut within 6-18 hours of pour at spacing 24-30 times slab thickness (8-10 feet on a 4-inch slab). Joint depth is 1/4 of slab thickness to control crack location.

howToSteps.name: Cure and barricade
howToSteps.text: The slab is barricaded to prevent traffic for 7 days minimum. A curing compound is sprayed or wet-cure plastic is laid down. Light vehicles are permitted after 7 days; full design strength is reached at 28 days.

content:
Quick Answer: Replacing a concrete driveway costs $7-$15 per square foot installed in 2026, all-in (demo + haul-off + new pour). A typical 600-square-foot 2-car driveway lands at $4,500-$10,000, with $4,500-$7,500 the most common quote band. Demolition alone is $1.50-$3.50 per square foot — most of the gap vs new installation. Repair beats replacement up to ~30 percent slab damage; above that, full replacement delivers better value.

A concrete driveway is one of the few residential concrete jobs almost every homeowner eventually faces — driveways have a finite service life, and at year 30-40 most reach the point where spot repair stops being worth the spend. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 replacement pricing, what's in the number, and when repair beats replacement.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed driveway, slab, patio, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, replacing a concrete driveway runs $7-$15 per square foot installed all-in (demo, haul-off, base prep, pour, and finishing). On a typical 600-square-foot 2-car driveway that's $4,200-$9,000, with $4,500-$7,500 the most common quote band. The replacement number is roughly 25-50 percent higher than a brand-new install on bare ground because demolition, haul-off, and additional base prep are not part of new-construction pricing. Demolition alone accounts for $1.50-$3.50 per square foot — most of the cost gap. Driveway replacement pricing is meaningfully lower than full repair-and-resurface combined work above roughly 30 percent slab damage; below that threshold, repair usually wins on cost.

2026 replacement pricing by driveway size

National typical installed pricing for full replacement (demolition + haul-off + new base prep + pour + finish), assuming a 4-inch slab on residential grade with wire mesh reinforcement, broom finish, and a single 8-foot control joint pattern.

Driveway sizeSq ftLow ($7/sf)Typical ($10/sf)High ($15/sf)
1-car (single, short)300$2,100$3,000$4,500
1-car (single, full)450$3,150$4,500$6,750
2-car (standard)600$4,200$6,000$9,000
2-car (deep)800$5,600$8,000$12,000
3-car (or long run)1,000$7,000$10,000$15,000
Long rural driveway1,500$10,500$15,000$22,500

The per-square-foot rate trends down as the driveway gets larger because fixed costs (mobilization, equipment setup, dump-fee minimums) amortize across more area. A 1,500-square-foot driveway typically prices closer to $8-$13 per square foot rather than the higher $13-$15 range that small driveways see.

What's in the replacement number

A driveway replacement quote should cover six line items. Here's how a typical $7,500 replacement on a 600-square-foot driveway breaks down:

Line itemTypical cost% of total
Demolition (break-up)$600-$1,20010-15%
Haul-off + dump fees$400-$9006-12%
Base prep (regrade + gravel)$500-$1,2008-15%
Forms + reinforcement$400-$9006-12%
Concrete material (delivered)$1,400-$2,10020-30%
Labor (pour + finish)$1,800-$2,80025-35%
Total$5,100-$9,100100%

Concrete material alone is 20-30 percent of the bill. The rest is labor and labor-related (demo, haul, prep, formwork, finishing). That ratio is the same shape as foundation work covered in the Cost of Concrete concrete foundation cost guide — concrete is the smaller half; everything that touches it is the larger half.

Replace vs repair: the 30 percent rule

The industry rule of thumb on most residential driveways: repair below 30 percent slab damage, replace above. Damage means visible cracking, spalling, settlement, or surface scaling — not hairline shrinkage cracks at control joints, which are expected.

When to repair

  • Cracks affecting less than 30 percent of slab area
  • Settlement limited to 1-2 slab sections (mudjacking or polyjacking at $4-$10 per square foot of affected area)
  • Surface scaling without structural cracking (resurface at $4-$8 per square foot)
  • Driveway is less than 15 years old and damage is localized

When to replace

  • Cracking, spalling, or settlement affecting more than 30 percent of slab
  • Slab is 25+ years old with multiple failure modes
  • Base failure (the driveway pumps or rocks under load — not a slab problem, a base problem)
  • Tree root displacement that has lifted slab sections more than 1 inch
  • Surface so deeply spalled that resurfacing wouldn't bond

For repair pricing in detail (crack injection vs polyjacking vs mudjacking vs full slab replacement), see the Cost of Concrete concrete repair cost guide.

Demo and haul-off explained

The demolition + haul-off phase is the single biggest reason replacement costs more than new-build pricing. The work splits into three distinct steps.

Mechanical break-up. A jackhammer (handheld pneumatic), hydraulic breaker (skid-steer mounted), or breaker attachment on a mini excavator is used to fracture the slab into 12-24 inch chunks. Reinforced slabs (rebar or mesh) take 20-40 percent longer than unreinforced because the steel has to be cut as it's exposed. Typical labor: 4-8 crew-hours on a 600-square-foot residential driveway.

Loading and hauling. Debris is loaded into a 10-yard or 20-yard dump trailer, or a roll-off container is dropped on-site for the duration. Concrete weighs roughly 4,000 pounds per cubic yard; a 600-square-foot 4-inch slab produces about 7.5 cubic yards / 15 tons of debris. Two trailer trips typical.

Disposal. Dump fees run $35-$120 per ton depending on metro. Clean concrete (no rebar) is significantly cheaper to dispose of than rebar-contaminated debris because clean concrete is recycled into aggregate. According to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, concrete is the most-recycled construction material by weight in the United States, with roughly 80 percent of demolished concrete entering the recycling stream.

How driveway replacement is done

  1. Mark and protect surrounding area. The crew marks utility locates, protects adjacent landscaping, lawn, and curb. Plastic or plywood covers anything that could be hit by debris during the break-up phase.
  2. Break up existing slab. The old slab is broken into manageable chunks with a jackhammer, hydraulic breaker, or skid-steer mounted breaker. Reinforcement (mesh or rebar) is exposed and cut as the slab comes apart.
  3. Haul off debris. Concrete debris is loaded into a dump trailer or roll-off container and hauled to a recycling yard or landfill. Disposal fees vary $35-$120 per ton depending on metro and whether the concrete is clean or rebar-contaminated.
  4. Rework base and grade. The exposed subgrade is inspected, regraded, and re-compacted. Soft spots are dug out and replaced; a 4-6 inch compacted gravel base is added or refreshed. Final grade slopes 1-2 percent away from the house for drainage.
  5. Set forms and reinforcement. Forms are set to mark the new slab edges. Wire mesh, fiber, or rebar reinforcement is placed per the agreed spec, supported on chairs to land mid-slab during the pour.
  6. Pour and finish. Ready-mix concrete is poured (typically 3,500-4,000 PSI for residential driveways), screeded level, bull-floated, edged, and finished — usually broom for traction, smooth trowel for sealed garage approaches, or stamped for decorative.
  7. Cut control joints. Control joints are saw-cut within 6-18 hours of pour at spacing 24-30 times slab thickness (8-10 feet on a 4-inch slab). Joint depth is 1/4 of slab thickness to control crack location.
  8. Cure and barricade. The slab is barricaded to prevent traffic for 7 days minimum. A curing compound is sprayed or wet-cure plastic is laid down. Light vehicles are permitted after 7 days; full design strength is reached at 28 days.

Upgrades that add real cost

Most replacement quotes come in at the standard spec (4-inch slab, wire mesh, 3,500 PSI, broom finish). Common upgrades and their installed-price impact:

UpgradeAdded costWhen it's worth it
Upgrade to 6-inch slab+$1-$2 / sq ftRV / truck / boat trailer traffic; expansive soils
Rebar instead of mesh+$0.50-$1.00 / sq ftFreeze-thaw climates; heavy load profile
Upgrade to 4,000+ PSI mix+$0.30-$0.60 / sq ftCold-climate / freeze-thaw resistance
Stamped or decorative finish+$4-$8 / sq ftResale-driven appearance upgrade
Permeable concrete (pervious)+$3-$8 / sq ftDrainage code requirements; stormwater rebates
Heated driveway (snow-melt)+$12-$25 / sq ftCold climates with steep grade or accessibility need
Sealer (initial)+$0.30-$0.60 / sq ftFreeze-thaw climates; always recommended on stamped

Stamped finish on a replacement is the most commonly chosen upgrade. The Cost of Concrete stamped vs regular concrete guide covers the upcharge in detail.

Regional pricing variation

Concrete driveway replacement pricing varies 30-50 percent between U.S. metros. Drivers: ready-mix concrete pricing ($130-$220 per cubic yard), local labor rates ($45-$120/hour), dump fees ($35-$120/ton), and climate-driven base prep requirements. According to the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, ready-mix delivered pricing has risen 18-24 percent nationally from 2023 to 2026, with cement clinker availability the primary driver.

Cheapest replacement pricing in 2026 sits in the South-Central U.S. (Texas, Oklahoma, Tennessee, Mississippi) at $6-$9 per square foot. Most expensive markets: San Francisco Bay, Seattle, Honolulu, Boston, New York at $11-$17 per square foot.

Frequently asked questions

How much does it cost to replace a 2-car concrete driveway in 2026?

A typical 2-car driveway (600 square feet) runs $4,500-$10,000 all-in for replacement in 2026 — including demo, haul-off, new base prep, and the new pour. Most quotes come in at $4,500-$7,500. Wider driveways, longer runs to the street, decorative finishes, or sloped sites push toward the upper end. Tear-out alone is $1,500-$2,500 of that total.

Is it cheaper to repair or replace a concrete driveway?

Repair is cheaper than replacement up to roughly 30 percent slab damage by area. Below that, crack injection, mudjacking, or partial slab replacement runs $1.50-$8 per square foot of affected area. Above 30 percent damage or where slab settlement is widespread, full replacement at $7-$15 per square foot delivers better total value because spot repairs continue to age while the unrepaired slab keeps failing.

How much does it cost just to remove a concrete driveway?

Concrete driveway removal alone runs $1.50-$3.50 per square foot in 2026 — roughly $900-$2,100 for a 600-square-foot driveway. The pricing includes mechanical breaking (jackhammer or skid-steer breaker), hauling debris, and dump-fee disposal. Reinforced concrete (rebar, mesh) costs roughly 15-25 percent more to remove than unreinforced. Pricing rises another 20-40 percent in metros with high dump fees (San Francisco, Seattle, New York).

Why is replacing a driveway more expensive than installing a new one?

A driveway replacement adds two cost categories that a new install doesn't have: demolition (mechanical break-up of the old slab) and haul-off disposal. Together they add $1.50-$3.50 per square foot — typically $1,000-$2,200 on a 2-car driveway. The base prep is also usually more involved on a replacement because the underlying base often needs rework after the old slab is pulled.

Can you pour new concrete over an old driveway?

You can in some cases, with a 2-3 inch concrete overlay (resurfacing) running $4-$8 per square foot. But overlays only work if the underlying slab is structurally sound — no settlement, no major cracks, no surface contamination. Most driveways past their service life have at least one of those issues, which is why true overlays are rarer than full replacements. According to the American Concrete Institute, overlays bonded to compromised substrate typically fail within 5-8 years.

How long does a concrete driveway last before it needs replacement?

A properly installed concrete driveway lasts 25-50 years before requiring full replacement, with 30-35 years the typical national average. Driveways in freeze-thaw climates fail earlier (25-30 years); those in warm climates with good base prep can pass 50. The Portland Cement Association cites proper base preparation and timely sealing as the two biggest determinants of whether a driveway hits the low end or high end of that range.

Should I add rebar when replacing my driveway?

For most residential driveways, wire mesh or synthetic fiber reinforcement is the cost-effective standard. Rebar is recommended for driveways supporting heavy trucks, RVs, or boat trailers; on expansive soils; or in freeze-thaw climates. Adding rebar to a typical 600-square-foot driveway runs $300-$600 more than wire mesh and significantly reduces hairline cracking over the slab's life.

How long does a driveway replacement take?

A standard 2-car driveway replacement takes 2-4 days of on-site work spread over 7-14 days. Day 1: demolition, haul-off, base prep. Day 2-3: forming, pour, finishing. Day 4+: cure. The slab is generally drivable by light vehicles after 7 days and full strength at 28 days. Total elapsed time from quote signature to drivable driveway is typically 3-6 weeks counting scheduling lead time.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 national replacement pricing: $7-$15 per square foot all-in. Typical 2-car driveway lands at $4,500-$10,000.
  • Demolition and haul-off add $1.50-$3.50 per square foot vs new install — that's the main reason replacement is more expensive than original construction.
  • Concrete material alone is only 20-30 percent of the bill; the rest is labor, demo, hauling, and base prep.
  • Repair beats replacement below 30 percent slab damage; above 30 percent, replace.
  • Standard upgrades worth considering at replacement: 6-inch slab on heavy-load driveways, rebar in freeze-thaw climates, sealer at finish.
  • Total elapsed time from signed quote to drivable driveway: 3-6 weeks (scheduling + 2-4 work days + 7 day cure).

Need a contractor whose replacement quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local driveway replacement pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
